segments.text: Hi everyone and welcome to the MCP Workshop series where we break down everything you need
to know to build intelligent AI agents for the AC industry.
This series is structured around four pillars.
Data, AI, API and MCP.
Each one is independent but together they form the foundation of any intelligent system.
We'll cover the theory behind each pillar and then move into hands-on implementation
where we build a full MCP system from scratch.
Step by step with real code and real Autodesk data.
Today we start with the first pillar, data.
Let's get the most important point out of the way.
AI is entirely dependent on data.
There is no way around it.
The quality of AI system is directly proportional to the quality of your data.
If your data is messy, your model will be messy.
And this is especially relevant in AC where data is often incomplete, heterogeneous and
scattered across disconnected systems.
You might have a rapid model that says one thing, a spreadsheet that says another,
and the PDF plan set that contradicts both.
So before we talk about AI, before we talk about APIs or MCP, we need to understand what
data we're working with and how to evaluate it.
There are six main categories of data that matter in our industry.
Numeric, text, audio, visual or 2D, video and geometric or 3D.
Each one behaves differently, its process differently and comes with its own set of challenges.
Let's go through them quickly.
Characteristic data is information expressed as numbers for measurement or calculation.
In AC, this includes cost data, planning data, telemetry from sensors and resource utilization
metrics.
This is often the easiest type of data to work with because it's inherently structured,
but it still needs to be accurate and consistent.
Text data is information conveyed through sequences of characters or words.
Texting site reports, contract documents, standards and project specifications.
Text is rich in context, but hard to process automatically.
A sentence like, the beam should be reinforced, carries meaning that's obvious to a human,
but requires natural language processing for a machine to interpret.
Audio data is information captured as variations in sound over time.
This covers meeting recordings, voice notes, noise monitoring and safety briefings on site.
Audio is temporal and dense.
One hour recording contains far more information than most people realize, and extracting useful
data from it requires transcription, filtering and context.
Visual or 2D data is represented as a flat grid of pixels.
This includes 2D CAD drawings, PDF plan sets, photographs and scan sketches.
Video text is further by adding the dimension of time.
Drone footage, progress images, side videos and training recordings all fall into this
category.
Both are powerful sources of information, but they're unstructured by nature and require
computer vision techniques to extract meaning.
The basic or 3D data is defined by points or shapes in three-dimensional space.
This is where rev it and beam models live, along with GIS models, lighter scans and point
clouds.
This is arguably the most valuable type of data in AC, but it's also the most complex.
A beam model isn't just geometry.
It's geometry plus metadata, relationships and context.
Now that we know what types of data exist, the next question is, how do we evaluate
whether our data is any good?
There are four key attributes.
First, accuracy.
Does the data correctly reflect real-world facts?
An accurate dataset hits the target.
A precise but inaccurate dataset clustered tightly but in the wrong place.
In AC this could be a model that shows dimensions consistently, but they're all 5 cm off from
the actual site.
Second, consistency.
Is the data represented identically across systems?
If one system calls an element A01 cube with dimensions in meters and another one calls
it cube A1 with dimensions in this emitters, you have a consistency problem.
One object, different representations.
Third, completeness.
Is all required data captured with non-necessary parts missing?
An incomplete dataset is like a puzzle with missing pieces.
You might be able to guess what goes there, but your AI system counts.
Fourth, accessibility.
Can authorized users actually locate, retrieve and use the data when they need it?
The log behind permissions, buried in legacy systems or stored in formats nobody can read
is effectively invisible.
It's also important to distinguish between internal and external data quality.
Internal data quality is about evaluating the data contained in your own systems.
You have full control over this.
You set the rules, the format, the naming conventions.
Full quality is about how your data interfaces with external systems.
Here you need to comply with industry standards and partner specifications.
You don't control the format, you adapt to it.
Another critical distinction is structured versus unstructured data.
Structured data is organized in defined format.
Tables, columns, fields, Excel spreadsheets, SQL databases, sensor measurements, easy
to query and connect across systems.
Unstructured data has no fixed format.
Emails, PDFs, images, videos, conversations, scan plans.
It's difficult to analyze automatically but often rich in context.
The good news is that unstructured data can be transformed into structured elements.
You can use automatic document classification, object detection or entity recognition to
extract names, places and numbers from free text.
Two more concepts to cover before we wrap up.
Data quantity is a balancing act.
Two little data limits analysis and reliability.
Too much data makes management and access complex.
A database with a million records can be less useful than a sample of 100,000 that covers
all observed situations.
Data should always be evaluated relative to coverage and intent and use.
Noise is the other side of the coin.
Noise refers to error news, inconsistent or random values that don't represent observed
reality.
It hides real trends and reduces reliability.
Sources include data entry errors, miscalibrated sensors, incomplete records and byte format
conversions.
Noise never disappears completely but it can be reduced through verification, outlier
detection and more importantly domain expertise.
Finally, let's look at the AC data pipeline.
This is the five stage process that takes raw data and makes it usable.
Collection, evaluation, structuring, cleaning and enrichment.
Each stage builds on the previous one and skipping any of them means your downstream AI
systems will suffer.
This pipeline is what connects the data pillar to everything else will cover in this series.
Clean, structured and rich data is what feeds the AI models, flows through the APIs and gets
exposed via MCP.
That's our first pillar covered.
Data is the foundation, everything else builds on.
Next time we'll move to the second pillar, AI.
We'll look at what types of AI exist, how they map to human capabilities and where they're
being applied in AC right now.
Leave your questions in the comments and I'll see you for the next one.
